Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to set aside time each day to read through the course materials, usually in the evenings after work. I preferred printing out readings or copying notes into my own documents so I could study without being online. I aimed for about 24 hours a week, but it rarely felt organized—more like fitting study time into whatever gaps I had.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd be really consistent, studying a few hours each evening. But then deadlines would pile up or I'd feel overwhelmed, and everything would shift to last-minute cramming. I tried to be regular, but life kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check what assignments were due or to download new materials. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about something and that would prompt me to go in. Honestly, I didn't log in just to browse—it was always purpose-driven, like grabbing a file or checking a deadline.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It became almost automatic—wake up, check what's new, download what I needed. But that routine broke down when I started feeling more stressed and sad about how the course was going. After that, logging in felt more like a chore than a habit.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and make my own summaries and notes. The videos were helpful too, even though I watched a lot of them—I'd often play them while taking handwritten notes. Having everything in one place made it easier to find what I needed.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion forums—I only posted once the whole semester. I felt too anxious about saying something wrong or looking stupid in front of everyone. I also skipped a lot of the optional practice activities because I was already struggling to keep up with the required work. It felt like there wasn't enough time for extras.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. I needed to see the practical value, like how reading this chapter would help me complete that assignment. When something felt abstract or disconnected from what I was being graded on, I'd push it aside. With limited time and energy, I had to be strategic.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them or face consequences. Optional stuff, even if it might help me learn better, always got deprioritized because the required work was already overwhelming. I wish I'd had more bandwidth for the optional resources.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download readings and print them out, then study from my notes at the library or at home without being connected. I also used some external YouTube videos to understand concepts better. The LMS was more like a pickup point for materials rather than where I actually learned.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours going through my printed notes and handwritten summaries, testing myself on concepts. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a friend from another class sometimes, quizzing each other, but that peer interaction wasn't captured. My real study life was mostly invisible.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a PDF or video once, download it, and then work with it for days or weeks without going back to the LMS. I'd watch a video, pause it, take notes, then review those notes multiple times offline. The single access didn't reflect how much I actually engaged with that material afterward.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. I was dealing with a lot of sadness and feeling like no matter how hard I tried, I wasn't getting the results I wanted. My attendance dropped because getting out of bed felt impossible some days. The CBT sessions helped a bit, but there were still stretches where engaging with anything academic felt like pushing through mud.
Q13: Definitely. When I was stressed or exhausted, I'd log in with good intentions but just stare at the screen. Competing responsibilities—work, family stuff—meant I sometimes had to choose between the LMS and sleep. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely because I didn't know where to start. It all compounded.
Q14: Many times. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through readings without actually absorbing anything because I was too tired or preoccupied. My eyes were on the screen, but I wasn't really there. Those sessions felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments even when I knew they were important, especially when I felt overwhelmed or sad. It's like my brain would freeze and I couldn't make myself start. The bigger or more complicated something seemed, the more I'd avoid it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming and rushing. Without a deadline, I'd keep pushing things off. The structure of due dates was both stressful and necessary—without them, I might not have submitted anything at all.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep trying. My assignment scores were actually pretty decent, which surprised me given everything else. But when I got my exam score back and it was so low, it was crushing. That negative feedback made it harder to engage afterward because I felt like my effort wasn't translating to success.
Q18: The course had a lot of flexibility, which sounds good but actually made it harder for me. With so many online modules and not much required attendance, I could easily fall behind without anyone noticing. I needed more structure and check-ins. The freedom to work at my own pace became freedom to procrastinate and struggle alone.
Q19: Having materials available to download was great for my learning style. But the sheer number of courses—eleven online courses—made it overwhelming to keep track of everything. The discussion forums felt intimidating and I never got comfortable there. More scaffolding or smaller milestones might have helped me stay on track better.
Q20: I think sometimes you're trying really hard to understand but you just can't pull it together into a finished product. You watch videos, read materials, take notes—all that activity—but when it comes to the assignment, you're stuck or confused or just out of time. High activity can mean struggling, not succeeding.
Q21: That makes sense to me because some people study efficiently offline. If someone reads fast, takes good notes once, and doesn't need to rewatch videos, their LMS activity would be low but their understanding could be solid. I wish that were more my experience—I had moderate activity but still failed because my exam performance didn't match my effort.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and how often, which gives a surface picture of engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you were actually focused or just clicking through. They also miss all the offline work, the emotional struggles, the real reasons behind the patterns. Numbers don't capture the full story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through—it's being mentally present and wanting to improve. For me, engagement also means feeling connected to the material and believing that your effort matters. This semester, I wanted to be engaged but often felt too defeated to fully get there.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting to stay afloat emotionally. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the sadness that made simple tasks feel impossible. It also wouldn't show that despite failing, I actually learned things and tried my best with the resources I had. My engagement was real, even if my grades don't reflect it.
